title: Känguru der Mathematik 2018 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2018_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: Alice draws lines between the beetles, starting with the beetle that has the fewest points and continuing each time to the beetle with one more point. Which figure is formed?
questions.solution: Connect the beetles in order of how many points they have, fewest first. Joining them in that order traces out figure D.
solution_steps: Put the beetles in order by their number of points.
Draw lines from one to the next in that order.
The line path forms figure D.

questions.question_text: The same number of kangaroos should be in both parks. How many kangaroos have to be moved from the left park to the right park?
questions.solution: Count both parks: the left has 13 and the right has 3. The difference is 10, and moving half of it (5) makes both parks have 8.
solution_steps: Left park has 13 kangaroos, right park has 3.
Difference = 13 − 3 = 10; move half of it.
Move 5 so both parks have 8.

questions.question_text: Which beetle has to fly away so that the remaining beetles have 20 dots altogether?
questions.solution: Add up all the dots on the five beetles: 27. To leave 20, the beetle that flies away must carry 27 − 20 = 7 dots.
solution_steps: Total dots on all beetles = 27.
To leave 20, remove 27 − 20 = 7 dots.
So the beetle with 7 points flies away.

questions.question_text: Peter draws his pattern exactly once more (repeated to the right). Which point lies on his drawing?
questions.solution: Continue the up-and-down pattern one more time across the grid. The repeated pattern passes exactly through point D.
solution_steps: Copy the pattern again starting where it ended.
Follow the line across the grid points.
It passes through point D.

questions.question_text: Theodor built a tower of discs and looks at it from above. How many discs does he see?
questions.solution: From above you can only see a disc if no wider disc sits above it. Going down the stack, 3 discs are each wider than everything above them, so 3 discs are visible.
solution_steps: From above, a disc is hidden if a wider disc is above it.
Find the discs wider than all discs above them.
There are 3 such discs.

questions.question_text: Two see-through sheets are placed on top of each other. Which pattern do you get?
questions.solution: Lay one sheet's lines over the other's without moving them. All the lines together make the pattern in option D.
solution_steps: Keep both sheets in place and overlap them.
Combine every line from both sheets.
The combined pattern is option D.

questions.question_text: To reach his bone, the dog follows the black line, turning right 3 times and left 2 times in total. Which path does he take?
questions.solution: Walk along each path and count the turns. Only path E has exactly 3 right turns and 2 left turns.
solution_steps: Follow each path and mark every turn as left or right.
Count the rights and the lefts.
Only path E gives 3 right and 2 left turns.

questions.question_text: Lisa needs exactly 3 pieces to complete her jigsaw. Which of the 4 pieces is left over?
questions.solution: Match the bumps and holes of the empty jigsaw spaces. Three pieces fit the gaps exactly, leaving piece A unused.
solution_steps: Look at the shape of the empty jigsaw spaces.
Fit the pieces whose bumps and holes match.
Three pieces fit, so piece A is left over.

questions.question_text: Charles cuts a rope into 3 equal pieces, then ties 1 knot in one piece, 2 in the next and 3 in the third. He lays them down in a random order. Which picture does he see?
questions.solution: The three pieces must show 1, 2 and 3 knots (in some order) and be the same length. Only picture B shows three equal ropes with 1, 2 and 3 knots.
solution_steps: The three ropes are equal in length.
They must carry 1, 2 and 3 knots in some order.
Only picture B matches that.

questions.question_text: How many of the hands pictured show a right hand?
questions.solution: Check each hand by the position of the thumb to decide if it is a right or left hand. Five of the pictured hands are right hands.
solution_steps: For each hand, use the thumb's side to tell right from left.
Count the ones that are right hands.
There are 5 right hands.

questions.question_text: The number of spots on each toadstool shows how many dwarfs fit under it. We see one side; the other side has the same number of spots. When it rains, 36 dwarfs try to hide. How many dwarfs get wet?
questions.solution: Each toadstool holds twice its visible spots (both sides). The visible spots add to 15, so the toadstools shelter 2 × 15 = 30 dwarfs. That leaves 36 − 30 = 6 dwarfs in the rain.
solution_steps: Each toadstool shelters double its visible spots (two equal sides).
Visible spots total 15, so shelter = 2 × 15 = 30 dwarfs.
Wet dwarfs = 36 − 30 = 6.

questions.question_text: You form two-digit numbers using the digits 2, 0, 1, 8. They must be bigger than 10 and smaller than 25, with two different digits. How many different numbers do you get?
questions.solution: The number must be between 11 and 24 with both digits from {2,0,1,8} and different. The tens digit can be 1 (giving 12, 18) or 2 (giving 20, 21). That makes 4 numbers.
solution_steps: Range is 11 to 24, digits from 2, 0, 1, 8, all different.
Tens digit 1: 12, 18. Tens digit 2: 20, 21.
That is 4 numbers in total.

questions.question_text: Alice has 3 white, 2 black and 2 grey pieces of paper. First she cuts every piece that is NOT black into two. Then she cuts every piece that is NOT white into two. How many pieces does she obtain in total?
questions.solution: Step 1 doubles the white and grey: white 3→6, grey 2→4, black stays 2 (12 pieces). Step 2 doubles every non-white: black 2→4, grey 4→8, white stays 6. Total = 6 + 4 + 8 = 18.
solution_steps: Start: 3 white, 2 black, 2 grey.
Cut non-black: white 3→6, grey 2→4 (black stays 2) → 12 pieces.
Cut non-white: black 2→4, grey 4→8 (white stays 6) → 6+4+8 = 18.

questions.question_text: Susi makes a pattern using ice-lolly sticks, each 5 cm long and 1 cm wide. How long is Susi's pattern?
questions.solution: Going along the pattern, horizontal sticks add their 5 cm length while vertical sticks add only their 1 cm width. Adding the lengths across the whole chain gives 21 cm.
solution_steps: A horizontal stick adds 5 cm; a vertical stick adds 1 cm of width.
Add the contributions of each stick along the pattern.
The total length is 21 cm.

questions.question_text: The road from Anna's to Mary's house is 16 km. The road from Mary's to John's is 20 km. The road from the crossing to Mary's house is 9 km. How long is the road from Anna's to John's house?
questions.solution: The two roads share a crossing. Anna to the crossing = 16 − 9 = 7 km. The crossing to John = 20 − 9 = 11 km. So Anna to John = 7 + 11 = 18 km.
solution_steps: Anna→crossing = 16 − 9 = 7 km (Mary is 9 km past the crossing).
Crossing→John = 20 − 9 = 11 km.
Anna→John = 7 + 11 = 18 km.
